query: role of TNF-α in activating pancreatic stellate cells

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pancreas uses oxidative and non-oxidative metabolism to process alcohol. The oxidative pathway leads to the formation of acetaldehyde, a reactive metabolite that causes detrimental effects in acinar cells through activation of stellate cells, increased expression of proinflammatory cytokines, and a decrease in NAD+/NADH ratios. The non-oxidative pathway requires the formation of fatty acid ethyl ester (FAEE) synthase, which leads to activation of key transcription factors, sustained increases in intracellular calcium, and inhibition of extracellular matrix proteins, ultimately leading to further cell injury [2] .

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Recent research suggests that alcohol diminishes the cell's ability to respond to calcium signaling. This reduced response disrupts the feedback mechanism and initiates a cycle of cellular damage, leading to cell death. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Carbon Tetrachloride Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: At the molecular level, CCl4 activates multiple factors, including tumor necrosis factor (TNF)α, transforming growth factors (TGF)-α and -ß in the cell, and nitric oxide (NO). These factors appear to direct the cell toward self-destruction or fibrosis. TNFα directs the cells toward apoptosis, whereas the TGFs directs the cells toward fibrosis. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Pancreas -- Structure and Function -- Cell Types
retrieved_docs.content: The pancreas also contains the islet of Langerhans, which contain the endocrine cells. Unlike the exocrine enzymes, which are secreted by exocytosis, the endocrine enzymes enter the bloodstream via a complex capillary network within the pancreatic blood flow. There are 4 types of endocrine cells (A cells produce glucagon, B cells produce insulin, D cells produce somatostatin, and F cells produce pancreatic polypeptide). Stellate cells are a direct formation of epithelial structures within the pancreas. In conditions like chronic pancreatitis, these cells promote inflammation and fibrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatic fibrosis is the deposition of extracellular matrix (ECM) in response to chronic liver injury by any etiology. The common pathway is initiated by hepatic stellate cells (HSC), which usually are vitamin A storing dormant cells found in space between sinusoids and hepatocytes. In response to chronic liver injury, HSC gets activated into proliferative fibrogenic myofibroblast and upregulates the expression of inflammatory receptors such as chemokine receptors, ICAM-1, and other inflammatory mediators by releasing chemokines and other leukocyte chemoattractants. This pro-inflammatory phase or initiation phase also changes gene and phenotypic expression of the liver cells, making them more responsive to these inflammatory cytokines, and perpetuation of activated HSC cells results in the accumulation of ECM and progressive fibrosis. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic tissue from humans with LADA and a rat model mimicking LADA were analyzed using immunohistochemistry and PCR. Predominant macrophage (CD68) infiltration was shown in the islet cells as opposed to T1DM, which showed CD8 lymphocytes. Islet groups exist with and without infiltration. The cell type shift translated to greater interleukin-1β cytokine secretion and decreased expression of TNF-α in T lymphocytes. Also, the proliferation marker of nuclear antigen and the anti-inflammatory cytokine interleukin 10 (IL-10) were elevated, while the apoptotic promoters caspase3 and TUNEL were diminished. The result was increased β-cell gene transcription, greater C-peptide levels, slower progression of β-cell destruction, and slower onset of LADA compared to T1DM. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Fibroblast -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Fibroblasts are the most common cell type represented in connective tissue. These cells produce a diverse group of products including collagen type I, III, and IV, proteoglycans, fibronectin, laminins, glycosaminoglycans, metalloproteinases, and even prostaglandins. In the adult body, fibroblasts remain in a quiescent form until stimuli activate protein synthesis and contractile mechanisms. These cells synthesize reorganize the ECM found in the skin, lung, heart, kidney, liver, eye, and other organs. The ECM is in constant communication with the surrounding cells as fibroblasts can secrete and respond to both autocrine and paracrine signals. [3] [4] Matrix reorganization occurs through a process of degradation and crosslinking enzymes, produced by fibroblasts, that are activated and regulated by pro-inflammatory cytokines and growth factors. Transcription growth factor-alpha and beta (TGF-A and TGF-B), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), epidermal growth factor (EGF), and tumor necrosis factor (TNF) all have implications in fibroblast regulation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Cirrhosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple cells play a role in liver cirrhosis, including hepatocytes and sinusoidal lining cells such as hepatic stellate cells (HSCs), sinusoidal endothelial cells (SECs), and Kupffer cells (KCs). HSCs form a part of the wall of the liver sinusoids, and their function is to store vitamin A. When these cells are exposed to inflammatory cytokines, they get activated, transform into myofibroblasts, and start depositing collagen, which results in fibrosis. SECs form the endothelial lining and are characterized by the fenestrations they make in the wall that allow the exchange of fluid and nutrients between the sinusoids and the hepatocytes. [4] Defenestration of the sinusoidal wall can happen secondary to chronic alcohol use and promote perisinusoidal fibrosis. [5] KCs are satellite macrophages that line the wall of the sinusoids as well. Studies from animal models have shown that they play a role in liver fibrosis by releasing harmful mediators when exposed to injurious agents and acting as antigen-presenting cells for viruses. [6] Hepatocytes are also involved in cirrhosis's pathogenesis, as damaged hepatocytes release reactive oxygen species and inflammatory mediators that can promote activating HSCs and liver fibrosis. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol also leads to premature activation of trypsinogen and other digestive and lysosomal enzymes within the acinar cells themselves; this causes the pancreatic tissue to auto-digest and leads to further inflammation. Another contributing mechanism is intra-acinar activation of factor-kB (NF-kB), a transcriptional activator, which further drives the inflammatory response, which can cause up to 50% of pancreatic tissue damage and lead to severe, fatal inflammatory responses [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Liver Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The stellate cells are thought to be the source of collagen in pathological conditions. Chronic liver injury activates hepatic stellate cells, which are activated and transformed into a myofibroblast-like phenotype. They then lay down the extracellular matrix. Chronic inflammation, cytokine production by damaged parenchymal cells, and disruption of the extracellular matrix are well-characterized stimuli to stellate cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Extrinsic Pathway of Apoptosis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Increasing the killing capabilities of the extrinsic pathway of apoptosis is desired in treating cancer, and a variety of anticancer drugs increase Fas receptor expression in some tumor cell lines. [15] The TNFRSF member TRAIL has shown some promise in being able to selectively kill cancer cells through binding to multiple receptors (DR4 and DR5). Yet, unfortunately, cancer cells have demonstrated the ability to obtain resistance to death by TRAIL. [16] Conversely, the extrinsic pathway of apoptosis is detrimental in other situations and can be the cause of disease. Extrinsic apoptosis mediated by CTLs has been postulated to cause the damaging effects of liver destruction in chronic viral hepatitis, and CTLs appear to be the cause of Type I diabetes, as the belief is the extrinsic apoptotic pathway initiated by CTLs eliminates the pancreatic beta cells in type I diabetes. [17] Finally, both NK cells and CTLs both contribute to graft-versus-host disease, a significant barrier to the success of bone marrow transplantation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Pancreas -- Clinical Significance -- Type 2 DM
retrieved_docs.content: Pathophysiology: Beta-cell dysfunction manifests in different ways: (1) reductions in insulin release, (2) changes in pulsatile insulin secretion, (3) an abnormality in the efficiency of proinsulin to insulin conversion, and (4) reduces the release of amylin. Insulin resistance is present in most patients with T2D. Insulin resistance is characterized by higher than expected plasma glucose level with the prevailing plasma insulin secretion. In patients with T2D insulin, stimulation fails to induce normal GLUT4 protein translocation to the sarcolemma in skeletal muscle membrane. Also, excessive production of free fatty acids and overexpression of TNF-alpha by adipocytes have been proposed as mechanisms for the development of insulin resistance. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Kupffer Cell -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Kupffer cell activation produces reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide, leading to oxidative stress in the liver. TLR4 activates interleukin-1 receptor-associated kinase (IRAK-1), which leads to the activation of nuclear factor kappa B (NF-kB). [20] The activation leads to numerous responses, which include the generation of superoxide and the production of cytokines; the result is liver damage and, eventually, loss of liver function. Kupffer cell activation has also been implicated in developing binge drinking-induced fatty liver disease. The process is mediated by TNF-alpha activation of lipolysis. Kupffer cells can activate inflammasomes, which trigger the activation caspase-1 and the production of IL-1beta, a pro-inflammatory mediator in alcoholic liver disease. Potential treatments for alcoholic liver disease are aimed at Kupffer cells’ role in the pathogenesis of the disease. Treatments for suppressing Kupffer cell activation and eliminating Kupffer cell cytotoxic products include antibiotics, probiotics, TNF-alpha, and IL-1beta inhibitors. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Milk Thistle -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, silybin also shows promise as an antifibrotic agent, which is attributable to its ability to decrease platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)-induced DNA synthesis in cells, which inhibits the transformation of stellate hepatocytes into myofibroblasts. By reducing myofibroblasts, silybin indirectly prevents the deposition of collagen fibers that lead to the progression of liver injury. [17] Finally, silybin has demonstrated an association with a significant reduction of TGF-β, a key regulator in the pathogenesis of liver fibrosis. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Reperfusion Injury -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Further studies done regarding retinal ischemia-reperfusion injuries have shown beneficial effects of pioglitazone. [12] Its use has been associated with the inactivation of glial cells and, in turn, the process of gliosis and fibrosis as well as inhibiting apoptosis. The NF-κB pathway is possibly involved in the process mentioned above. Superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione are some of the free radicle scavengers involved in the detoxification of the reactive oxygen species. The safety and efficacy of using recombinant; monoclonal antibodies against CD18 subunit of beta-2 integrin receptors in reducing leukocyte adhesion to endothelial linings are being studied. Animal studies using various methods of modulating the cytokine response have shown beneficial effects from modulation of IL-1 and TNF. [13] [14] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2918
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells represent the abnormal cellular component that defines and drives the disease known as السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that the presence of these atypical, cancerous cells is the hallmark of this specific malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a classification that encompasses several distinct disorders of pancreatic endocrine function. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes mellitus without any associated complications and represents the basic metabolic disturbance of chronic hyperglycemia. It also comprises اعتلال عصبي سكري, a chronic diabetic neuropathy in which prolonged hyperglycemia damages peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. The category further lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive atrophy of one side of the facial tissues that can be observed in some patients with endocrine pancreatic disease. فرط أنسولين الدم is another child condition, describing an excess of circulating insulin that may arise from insulin‑producing tumors (insulinoma) or other dysregulation of pancreatic β‑cell activity. Finally, ورم غلوكاغوني refers to a glucagon‑secreting pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma) that causes hyperglycemia and a characteristic clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of pancreatic endocrine disorders—from uncomplicated diabetes to its neuropathic complications, hormonal excess states, and neoplastic growths—underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) are clinically associated with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), indicating that patients with non‑diabetic pancreatic conditions often present with abdominal pain as a key clinical feature.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1980
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam, a bile‑acid sequestrant used to lower cholesterol levels, is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), indicating that this medication should not be prescribed to individuals suffering from this inflammatory pancreatic disease due to safety concerns.
